title: Brown butter chocolate chip cookies
subtitle: Nutty, caramel-edged cookies with toasted butter
excerpt: Browning the butter before making the dough adds a nutty, caramel depth that lifts these cookies above the standard chocolate chip. The technique takes 5 extra minutes and makes a significant difference to the flavour.
sourceNotes: The technique of browning butter (beurre noisette) for cookies was popularised in American food writing by New York Times recipe developer Melissa Clark and further developed by Dorie Greenspan in Dorie's Cookies (2016). The method is an adaptation of the standard Toll House cookie formula (Ruth Wakefield, 1938) with the single change of browning the fat before use, producing Maillard compounds that standard melted or creamed butter cannot develop.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked cookies in a sealed bag for up to 3 months. Or freeze unbaked dough balls; bake from frozen adding 3 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely. Keep ball weight consistent at 35 g for predictable spread and bake time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough improves with 24 to 72 hours in the fridge. Longer cold rest develops more complex flavour.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf, one tray at a time
baking.decoratingTechnique: flaked sea salt scattered over before baking

glossaryTerms.definition: French for 'hazelnut butter'. Butter that has been melted and cooked until the milk solids turn golden brown and the fat smells of toasted nuts and caramel. The colour and aroma come from Maillard reactions in the milk proteins.

content.text: Watch the butter closely during browning: it goes from melted to foaming to golden to burnt in about 2 minutes once it begins to colour. Remove it from the heat the moment the foam dies down and you see golden-brown specks at the bottom of the pan. Pour immediately into a cool bowl to stop the cooking.

Rest the dough in the fridge for at least 1 hour before baking. The cold rest solidifies the fat and allows the flour to hydrate fully. Cookies baked from room-temperature dough spread too thin and lace at the edges.
Brown the butter and make the dough

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a light-coloured saucepan over a medium heat. Continue cooking, stirring occasionally, until the foam subsides and the butter turns deep golden brown with a nutty smell, about 5 to 7 minutes from melting. This is
. Pour immediately into a large heatproof bowl and leave to cool for 15 minutes.

Whisk the {{light-brown-sugar}} and {{caster-sugar}} into the cooled brown butter until combined. Add the {{eggs}} (whole egg plus yolk) and {{vanilla-extract}} and whisk until the mixture lightens slightly. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}}, then the {{chocolate-chips}}. Refrigerate the dough for at least 1 hour (or up to 72 hours).

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Scoop the cold dough into balls of about 35 g each. Place on the trays spaced 6 cm apart. Scatter a few flakes of {{flaked-sea-salt}} over each ball. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the edges are set and golden but the centres are still soft and slightly domed. Cool on the tray for 10 minutes before transferring to a rack.
